content:
# Genesis Chapter 6 -- The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 4r-4v | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_006_page_001.jpg, GEN_006_page_002.jpg
**Additional Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) — LXX | **View Vaticanus online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0008

---

**6:1** And it came to pass [וַיְהִי] when the adam [הָאָדָם] began to multiply [לָרֹב] upon the face [עַל-פְּנֵי] of the ground [הָאֲדָמָה], and daughters [בָּנוֹת] were born to them —

**6:2** and the sons of God [בְּנֵי-הָאֱלֹהִים] saw the daughters of the adam [בְּנוֹת הָאָדָם] that they were good [טֹבֹת], and they took [וַיִּקְחוּ] for themselves wives [נָשִׁים] from all that they chose [אֲשֶׁר בָּחָרוּ].

**6:3** And YHWH said: My spirit [רוּחִי] shall not strive/abide [לֹא-יָדוֹן] in the adam [בָאָדָם] forever [לְעֹלָם], in that [בְּשַׁגַּם] he also [הוּא] is flesh [בָשָׂר]; and his days [וְהָיוּ יָמָיו] shall be one hundred and twenty years [מֵאָה וְעֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה].

**6:4** The Nephilim [הַנְּפִלִים] were in the earth in those days [בַּיָּמִים הָהֵם], and also afterward [וְגַם אַחֲרֵי-כֵן], when the sons of God [בְּנֵי הָאֱלֹהִים] came in to the daughters of the adam [בְּנוֹת הָאָדָם], and they bore to them [וְיָלְדוּ לָהֶם]: those were the mighty men [הַגִּבֹּרִים] who were from of old [אֲשֶׁר מֵעוֹלָם], the men of name [אַנְשֵׁי הַשֵּׁם].

**6:5** And YHWH saw [וַיַּרְא יְהוָה] that the evil of the adam [כִּי רַבָּה רָעַת הָאָדָם] was great in the earth [בָּאָרֶץ], and that every inclination [וְכָל-יֵצֶר] of the thoughts [מַחְשְׁבֹת] of his heart [לִבּוֹ] was only evil [רַק רַע] all the day [כָּל-הַיּוֹם].

**6:6** And YHWH was grieved [וַיִּנָּחֶם יְהוָה] that he had made [כִּי-עָשָׂה] the adam [אֶת-הָאָדָם] in the earth, and he was grieved to his heart [וַיִּתְעַצֵּב אֶל-לִבּוֹ].

**6:7** And YHWH said: I will wipe away [אֶמְחֶה] the adam [אֶת-הָאָדָם] whom I have created [אֲשֶׁר-בָּרָאתִי] from the face of the ground [מֵעַל פְּנֵי הָאֲדָמָה] — from adam to beast [מֵאָדָם עַד-בְּהֵמָה], to creeping thing [עַד-רֶמֶשׂ] and to the flying-things of the heavens [וְעַד-עוֹף הַשָּׁמָיִם] — for I am grieved [כִּי נִחַמְתִּי] that I made them [כִּי עֲשִׂיתִם].

**6:8** But Noah [וְנֹחַ] found favor [מָצָא חֵן] in the eyes of YHWH [בְּעֵינֵי יְהוָה].

**6:9** These are the generations of Noah [אֵלֶּה תּוֹלְדֹת נֹחַ]. Noah was a righteous man [אִישׁ צַדִּיק], blameless [תָּמִים] in his generations [בְּדֹרֹתָיו]; with the-God [אֶת-הָאֱלֹהִים] Noah walked [הִתְהַלֶּךְ-נֹחַ].

**6:10** And Noah begot three sons [שְׁלֹשָׁה בָנִים]: Shem [שֵׁם], Ham [חָם], and Yaphet [יָפֶת].

**6:11** And the earth [הָאָרֶץ] was corrupt [וַתִּשָּׁחֵת] before God [לִפְנֵי הָאֱלֹהִים], and the earth was filled with violence [וַתִּמָּלֵא הָאָרֶץ חָמָס].

**6:12** And God saw [וַיַּרְא אֱלֹהִים] the earth, and behold [וְהִנֵּה] it was corrupt [נִשְׁחָתָה], for all flesh [כִּי-הִשְׁחִית כָּל-בָּשָׂר] had corrupted its way [אֶת-דַּרְכּוֹ] upon the earth.

**6:13** And God said to Noah [וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים לְנֹחַ]: The end of all flesh [קֵץ כָּל-בָּשָׂר] has come before me [בָּא לְפָנַי], for the earth is filled with violence [כִּי-מָלְאָה הָאָרֶץ חָמָס] through them [מִפְּנֵיהֶם]; and behold I am about to destroy them [מַשְׁחִיתָם] with the earth [אֶת-הָאָרֶץ].

**6:14** Make for yourself an ark [עֲשֵׂה לְךָ תֵּבַת] of gopher-wood [עֲצֵי-גֹפֶר]; rooms [קִנִּים] you shall make in the ark [תַּעֲשֶׂה אֶת-הַתֵּבָה], and you shall cover it [וְכָפַרְתָּ אֹתָהּ] within and without [מִבַּיִת וּמִחוּץ] with pitch [בַּכֹּפֶר].

**6:15** And this [וְזֶה] is how you shall make it [אֲשֶׁר תַּעֲשֶׂה אֹתָהּ]: three hundred cubits [שְׁלֹשׁ מֵאוֹת אַמָּה] the length of the ark [אֹרֶךְ הַתֵּבָה], fifty cubits [חֲמִשִּׁים אַמָּה] its width [רָחְבָּהּ], and thirty cubits [וּשְׁלֹשִׁים אַמָּה] its height [קוֹמָתָהּ].

**6:16** A tsohar [צֹהַר] you shall make for the ark [תַּעֲשֶׂה לַתֵּבָה], and to a cubit [וְאֶל-אַמָּה] you shall finish it [תְּכַלֶּנָּה] from above [מִלְמַעְלָה]; and the door [וּפֶתַח] of the ark [הַתֵּבָה] you shall set [תָּשִׂים] in its side [בְּצִדָּהּ]; with lower [תַּחְתִּיִּים], second [שְׁנִיִּם], and third [שְׁלִשִׁים] decks you shall make it [תַּעֲשֶׂהָ].

**6:17** And I — behold [וַאֲנִי הִנְנִי] — I am bringing [מֵבִיא] the flood [אֶת-הַמַּבּוּל], waters [מַיִם] upon the earth [עַל-הָאָרֶץ], to destroy [לְשַׁחֵת] all flesh [כָּל-בָּשָׂר] in which is the breath of life [אֲשֶׁר-בּוֹ רוּחַ חַיִּים] from under the heavens [מִתַּחַת הַשָּׁמָיִם]; everything [כֹּל] that is in the earth [אֲשֶׁר-בָּאָרֶץ] shall expire [יִגְוָע].

**6:18** And I will establish [וַהֲקִמֹתִי] my covenant [אֶת-בְּרִיתִי] with you [אִתָּךְ]; and you shall come [וּבָאתָ] into the ark [אֶל-הַתֵּבָה] — you [אַתָּה] and your sons [וּבָנֶיךָ] and your wife [וְאִשְׁתְּךָ] and your sons' wives [וּנְשֵׁי-בָנֶיךָ] with you [אִתָּךְ].

**6:19** And from all the living [מִכָּל-הָחַי], from all flesh [מִכָּל-בָּשָׂר], two from all [שְׁנַיִם מִכֹּל] you shall bring [תָּבִיא] into the ark [אֶל-הַתֵּבָה], to keep alive [לְהַחֲיֹת] with you [אִתָּךְ]; male and female [זָכָר וּנְקֵבָה] they shall be [יִהְיוּ].

**6:20** From the flying-things [מֵהָעוֹף] according to their kind [לְמִינֵהוּ], and from the beasts [וּמִן-הַבְּהֵמָה] according to their kind [לְמִינָהּ], from every creeping thing of the ground [מִכֹּל רֶמֶשׂ הָאֲדָמָה] according to its kind [לְמִינֵהוּ], two from all [שְׁנַיִם מִכֹּל] shall come to you [יָבֹאוּ אֵלֶיךָ] to keep alive [לְהַחֲיוֹת].

**6:21** And you [וְאַתָּה] — take for yourself [קַח-לְךָ] from all food [מִכָּל-מַאֲכָל] that is eaten [אֲשֶׁר יֵאָכֵל], and gather it to yourself [וְאָסַפְתָּ אֵלֶיךָ], and it shall be for you [וְהָיָה לְךָ] and for them [וְלָהֶם] for food [לְאָכְלָה].

**6:22** And Noah did [וַיַּעַשׂ נֹחַ] according to all [כְּכֹל] that God commanded him [אֲשֶׁר צִוָּה אֹתוֹ אֱלֹהִים] — so he did [כֵּן עָשָׂה].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### On 6:1-2 — The Adam / Ground Linkage

The opening clause deploys the double resonance established in Genesis 2-5. הָאָדָם (the adam) multiplies upon פְּנֵי הָאֲדָמָה (the face of the ground). The adam-ground pairing, sounded throughout the Primeval History, is reactivated here at its darkest moment: the very proliferation that was the blessing of 1:28 (פְּרוּ וּרְבוּ) now produces the conditions for judgment.

The בְּנוֹת הָאָדָם ("daughters of the adam") in v. 2 picks up the same word — הָאָדָם, with the article — making the contrast with בְּנֵי-הָאֱלֹהִים ("sons of God") stark and structural. The human/divine boundary is encoded in the grammar of the definite article: הָאָדָם is mortal, creaturely, earthy; הָאֱלֹהִים is the divine.

### On 6:2 — "Sons of God" (בְּנֵי-הָאֱלֹהִים)

This phrase is one of the most contested in the Hebrew Bible. Three main interpretive traditions exist:

1. **Divine beings / divine council members** — the most natural reading of the Hebrew. בְּנֵי אֱלֹהִים in Job 1:6, 2:1, and 38:7 unambiguously denotes heavenly beings. The marriage of these beings with human women would then represent an illicit transgression of the cosmic boundary between divine and human. The Book of Enoch (1 En. 6-11) develops this reading extensively, identifying these figures as the Watchers (עִירִין).

2. **"Sons of rulers/judges"** — a rabbinic interpretation (cf. Targum Onqelos: בְּנֵי רַבְרְבַיָּא, "sons of nobles") that domesticates the passage by equating אֱלֹהִים with human rulers. Linguistically strained.

3. **Sethites vs. Cainites** — a patristic reading (Augustine, *City of God* XV) identifying the "sons of God" as the godly line of Seth marrying into the Cainite line. Exegetically harmonizing but without grounding in the text's own vocabulary.

**EMET renders the phrase literally without resolution.** The text says בְּנֵי-הָאֱלֹהִים. That is what it says.

**LXX (Vaticanus):** οἱ υἱοὶ τοῦ θεοῦ — "the sons of God," a literal rendering that preserves the ambiguity. Some LXX manuscripts (notably Alexandrinus and other witnesses) read instead οἱ ἄγγελοι τοῦ θεοῦ — "the angels of God" — an interpretive expansion that resolves toward the divine-beings reading. Vaticanus retains υἱοί.

**DSS:** 4Q417 and related texts from Qumran treat the בְּנֵי אֱלֹהִים as angelic beings, consistent with the Enochic tradition.

### On 6:3 — The Verb יָדוֹן (Yadon)

לֹא-יָדוֹן רוּחִי בָאָדָם לְעֹלָם is one of the most philologically vexed clauses in the Torah. The verb יָדוֹן occurs nowhere else in Biblical Hebrew in this form, making it a hapax in this conjugation. Several derivations have been proposed:

1. **From דִּין (din, "to judge, strive, contend")** — "My spirit shall not contend/strive in the adam forever." This is followed by many traditional commentators and modern translations (NRSV: "shall not abide"). The Niphal or Qal of דִּין would yield "strive, plead."

2. **From נוּד (nud, "to wander, remain")** — through metathesis or textual corruption: "My spirit shall not remain/abide." Several emendation proposals.

3. **From שָׁדַד (shaddai root) via a corruption** — speculative; not widely accepted.

4. **LXX:** οὐ μὴ καταμείνῃ τὸ πνεῦμά μου ἐν τοῖς ἀνθρώποις τούτοις εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα — "My spirit shall not remain in these people forever." The LXX translates with καταμένω ("to remain, abide"), suggesting the translator understood the sense as "abide/remain" rather than "strive/contend." This favors reading #2.

**EMET preserves the difficulty** in the translation: "shall not strive/abide." Both valences are present in the Hebrew; choosing one erases the other.

The phrase בְּשַׁגַּם ("in that / because also") is itself contested. It combines the preposition בְּ + שֶׁ (conjunction) + גַּם ("also"). The unusual compound form has led some scholars to propose a wordplay with the name שַׁגָּם, an alternative name tradition, but this remains speculative. The sense is: "because he also is flesh" — the divine spirit withdraws because the adam has become wholly identified with flesh (בָשָׂר).

**120 years:** The limitation of human lifespan to 120 years has been interpreted two ways: (a) the remaining lifespan until the flood — a decreed countdown; (b) a new limit on individual human life. The context in Genesis 6 favors reading (a), but the 120-year figure has resonance with Moses' death at age 120 (Deut. 34:7) — a Mosaic life that exactly fills the newly decreed human maximum.

### On 6:4 — The Nephilim (הַנְּפִלִים)

The noun נְפִלִים is transliterated, not translated. Its derivation is disputed:

1. **From נָפַל (napal, "to fall")** — "the fallen ones." This supports the reading of the בְּנֵי-הָאֱלֹהִים as fallen divine beings.

2. **From the Hiphil causative** — "those who cause others to fall," i.e., warriors who make others fall. This gives the meaning "mighty fallen warriors."

3. **The root is uncertain.** The form could be a Qal participle (fallen ones) or a noun of unknown origin.

The text makes a grammatical distinction: the Nephilim were in the earth בַּיָּמִים הָהֵם ("in those days") AND וְגַם אַחֲרֵי-כֵן ("and also after that"). This suggests the narrator is aware of a later tradition of Nephilim, consistent with Num. 13:33, where the Israelite spies report giants (Nephilim) in Canaan. The Nephilim are therefore not identical with the offspring of the sons of God; they are already present when the offspring are born. The offspring are instead identified as הַגִּבֹּרִים ("the mighty ones/heroes") and אַנְשֵׁי הַשֵּׁם ("men of name/renown/fame").

**LXX (Vaticanus):** οἱ γίγαντες — "the giants." The LXX renders נְפִלִים as γίγαντες throughout (Gen. 6:4; Num. 13:33). This is an interpretive choice that conflates the Nephilim with the Titans/giants of Greek tradition and has had enormous influence on Jewish and Christian reception. The translation of הַגִּבֹּרִים that follows is also οἱ γίγαντες in some manuscripts, compressing the two distinct Hebrew terms into one Greek one.

**DSS:** The Dead Sea Scrolls contain 4QEnGiants (fragments of the Book of Giants, also 1 En. 6-11), which develops the Nephilim tradition into an extensive mythology. 4Q531 and related texts name individual Nephilim and treat them as gigantic, violent beings whose corruption provoked the flood.

### On 6:5-7 — God's Grief and the Nacham Wordplay

The verb נָחַם (nacham) is the pivot of a devastating literary structure that spans Genesis 5-6:

- **5:29**: Lamech names his son נֹחַ (Noach), saying יְנַחֲמֵנוּ — "he will comfort/relieve us [nacham]" from the curse of the ground.
- **6:6**: וַיִּנָּחֶם יְהוָה — "And YHWH was grieved/repented [nacham]" that he had made the adam.
- **6:7**: כִּי נִחַמְתִּי — "for I am grieved/repent [nacham]" that I made them.

The name Noah (נֹחַ) is woven between two uses of the root that should be his comfort-word — but the word now denotes divine grief. The Hebrew narrator has constructed a dark irony: the very man named "comfort" stands embedded in a narrative of divine anguish. The word נָחַם carries in Biblical Hebrew both "to be comforted" and "to grieve/repent" — the semantic range itself enacts the ambivalence. Noah is the comfort of the nacham.

The verb וַיִּתְעַצֵּב ("and he was grieved/pained") in 6:6 is from עָצַב, the same root used in 3:16-17 for the pain of the woman in childbirth and the toil of the adam on the ground. YHWH's grief over humanity is expressed in the same vocabulary as humanity's post-Eden condition. The Creator shares the creature's suffering-word.

### On 6:6 — "And He Was Grieved to His Heart" (וַיִּתְעַצֵּב אֶל-לִבּוֹ)

The preposition אֶל ("to/toward/unto") rather than בְּ ("in") is noteworthy. The grief moves toward the heart — it is directed, purposive grief, not passive pain. YHWH's heart is the destination of this grief. This anthropopathic language is theologically dense: the narrator does not soften it, qualify it, or spiritualize it. It stands.

The LXX renders: καὶ ἐνεθυμήθη ὁ θεὸς ὅτι ἐποίησεν τὸν ἄνθρωπον ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς, καὶ διενοήθη — "and God considered that he had made man upon the earth, and he pondered." The LXX softens the grief into cognitive reflection — "pondered" (διενοήθη) rather than grief-to-the-heart. This is a significant theological shift: the Hebrew God grieves; the LXX God contemplates.

### On 6:7 — The Scope of Judgment

The wipe-out extends מֵאָדָם עַד-בְּהֵמָה עַד-רֶמֶשׂ וְעַד-עוֹף הַשָּׁמָיִם — "from adam to beast, to creeping thing and to flying-things of the heavens." The structure reverses the creation order of Genesis 1 (where the sequence runs from the heavens downward to humanity). Here the sequence runs from adam upward toward the birds — a creation-undoing in reverse sequence. The flood is de-creation.

The verb אֶמְחֶה ("I will wipe away") from מָחָה, "to blot out, wipe clean," is precise: the earth will be wiped clean like a slate.

### On 6:8 — Chen / Noach: The Anagram of Grace

וְנֹחַ מָצָא חֵן בְּעֵינֵי יְהוָה

The two Hebrew words are: נֹחַ (Noach) and חֵן (chen, "grace, favor"). Read in reverse, נֹחַ spells חֵן and חֵן spells נֹחַ. This is not coincidental — it is the kind of deliberate reversal that Hebrew narrative delights in. Noah (נֹחַ) found chen (חֵן): the man IS the grace read backwards. The name contains its gift.

The phrase בְּעֵינֵי יְהוָה ("in the eyes of YHWH") returns the narrative to seeing — the same root רָאָה that runs through vv. 1-5 (the sons of God "saw" the daughters; YHWH "saw" the evil). But here the seeing is redemptive.

### On 6:9 — The Third Toledot Formula

אֵלֶּה תּוֹלְדֹת נֹחַ — "These are the generations of Noah." This is the third toledot formula in Genesis (after 2:4 and 5:1). The word תּוֹלְדֹת appears here without סֵפֶר ("scroll"), unlike 5:1. The formula marks a structural unit.

**Three descriptions of Noah:**
1. אִישׁ צַדִּיק — "a righteous man." צַדִּיק is the standard word for legal/moral righteousness: conformity to a standard.
2. תָּמִים — "blameless/complete/wholesome." From תֹּם, wholeness or integrity. The same word used of sacrificial animals without blemish. It connotes undividedness.
3. הִתְהַלֶּךְ-נֹחַ אֶת-הָאֱלֹהִים — "Noah walked with the-God." The identical phrase used of Enoch in 5:22 and 5:24 (הִתְהַלֵּךְ אֶת-הָאֱלֹהִים). The Hitpael of הָלַךְ: reflexive-iterative, "walked about," "kept walking." This aligns Noah with Enoch — the man of walking-with-God who did not die.

**בְּדֹרֹתָיו — "in his generations":** This phrase is either laudatory (he was blameless among the corrupt people of his time) or limiting (he was blameless relative to his generation, implying he might not have met an absolute standard). Rashi takes the limiting view; most of the tradition takes the laudatory view. The text does not resolve it. EMET preserves the ambiguity: "in his generations."

**LXX:** Νωε δὲ εὗρεν χάριν ἐναντίον κυρίου τοῦ θεοῦ... Νωε ἄνθρωπος δίκαιος τέλειος ὢν ἐν τῇ γενεᾷ αὐτοῦ· τῷ θεῷ εὐηρέστησεν Νωε — "Noah pleased God." The LXX uses εὐηρέστησεν ("was well-pleasing to") instead of "walked with" — a translation that softens the intimacy of the walking metaphor into ethical approval. Vaticanus confirms this reading.

### On 6:11-12 — The Verb שָׁחַת and the Violence (חָמָס)

The root שָׁחַת ("to corrupt, destroy, ruin") appears three times in vv. 11-12 in rapid succession: וַתִּשָּׁחֵת ("and it was corrupted"), נִשְׁחָתָה ("it was corrupt"), הִשְׁחִית כָּל-בָּשָׂר ("all flesh had corrupted"). Then in v. 13: מַשְׁחִיתָם ("I am about to destroy them"). And in v. 17: לְשַׁחֵת ("to destroy"). The verb that describes human corruption is the same verb YHWH uses for the judgment: the earth is destroyed by the same act through which it destroys itself. The judgment mirrors the crime in its very vocabulary.

חָמָס ("violence, wrong, injustice") is the key diagnostic. It appears in v. 11 and v. 13. חָמָס is not merely physical violence; in the Hebrew prophetic tradition it denotes systemic injustice, oppression, and violation of social order (cf. Amos 3:10, Hab. 1:2-3, Ezek. 28:16). The earth is filled not merely with bloodshed but with the corruption of right order.

**Note:** חָמָס (hamas) in Hebrew means "violence/injustice" — entirely unrelated to any modern proper noun.

### On 6:13 — "The End of All Flesh Has Come Before Me"

קֵץ כָּל-בָּשָׂר בָּא לְפָנַי — literally "the end of all flesh has come before me." The word קֵץ ("end, limit") is the same root later used in apocalyptic literature (Daniel) for the eschatological end. Here it is concrete and immediate: the end of every living thing has arrived in the divine presence as a verdict.

### On 6:14 — Gopher Wood (עֲצֵי-גֹפֶר): Hapax Legomenon

גֹּפֶר appears nowhere else in the Hebrew Bible. It is an absolute hapax legomenon. Proposed identifications:

1. **Cypress** (most common in translations) — based on cognate languages and the ancient use of cypress in shipbuilding.
2. **Cedar** — used in other ancient Near Eastern flood traditions (the Gilgamesh Epic uses cedar and other woods).
3. **Resinous wood / pitch-wood** — some link גֹּפֶר to the word for pitch (כֹּפֶר, used in the same verse), suggesting a resin-rich wood.
4. **Unknown** — the honest answer. No ancient version is certain. The LXX translates ξύλα τετράγωνα ("squared timbers"), interpreting by shape rather than species. The Vulgate has "lignis laevigatis" (smoothed wood). Neither resolves the identity.

**EMET transliterates: gopher-wood.** The text does not know what we should translate, and neither should we pretend otherwise.

The verb כָּפַר ("to cover with pitch") in v. 14 (וְכָפַרְתָּ אֹתָהּ בַּכֹּפֶר) is the same root as the noun כֹּפֶר ("pitch") and — importantly — as the theological verb כִּפֵּר ("to atone/cover"). The ark is covered (kafar) with pitch (kofer) using the verb that will later denote atonement. Whether this is deliberate theological wordplay or incidental etymological proximity, the connection is present in the Hebrew and not recoverable in translation.

### On 6:15 — The Dimensions

300 × 50 × 30 cubits. A cubit (אַמָּה) is approximately 18 inches / 45 cm, giving approximate dimensions of 450 × 75 × 45 feet or 137 × 23 × 14 meters. The length-to-width ratio of 6:1 is notably close to modern ship design ratios for stability (a ratio of 6:1 to 7:1 is standard). Whether intentional or not, the narrative presents dimensions that function as a seaworthy hull.

**LXX:** The LXX gives 300 × 50 × 30 πήχεις (cubits) — identical to MT. No divergence here.

### On 6:16 — Tsohar (צֹהַר): Another Lexical Problem

צֹהַר is of uncertain meaning. It appears in this verse and possibly in Genesis 43:16 (in a different context, צָהֳרַיִם meaning "noon/midday brightness"). Proposed meanings:

1. **Window / opening** — the most common translation. The ark needs light and ventilation.
2. **Roof / covering** — a structural element at the top.
3. **Translucent stone / skylight** — medieval Jewish tradition (citing Talmud Sanhedrin 108b) speaks of a precious stone that gave light inside the ark, a midrashic expansion not warranted by the text but perhaps preserving a memory of the original sense.
4. **Pitch-seam / caulk-line** — highly speculative.

The instruction "to a cubit you shall finish it from above" (וְאֶל-אַמָּה תְּכַלֶּנָּה מִלְמַעְלָה) suggests the tsohar tapers or culminates at one cubit's measurement at its top edge. This supports the sense of a sloped or narrowing roof with a gap or opening at the ridge.

**EMET transliterates: tsohar.** To render it "window" is to choose; to render it "roof" is to choose differently. The Hebrew has not chosen for us.

**LXX:** ἐπισυνάγων ποιήσεις αὐτήν — "making it gathered/tapering you shall make it." The Greek is itself obscure, suggesting the LXX translator was equally uncertain.

### On 6:17 — מַבּוּל (Mabbul): The Flood

The word מַבּוּל appears only in Genesis 6-9 and Psalm 29:10, where YHWH "sat enthroned at the mabbul." It is not the ordinary Hebrew word for flood (שֶׁטֶף or נָהָר). Its etymology is debated; it may be related to Akkadian *nabālu* ("destruction") or may be an independent Hebrew formation. It denotes specifically the cosmic primordial deluge — a technical term for this singular event.

רוּחַ חַיִּים ("breath/spirit of life") in v. 17 echoes 2:7, where YHWH breathed נִשְׁמַת חַיִּים ("the breath of life") into the adam. The same life-breath that constituted humanity is now the marker of what will be destroyed.

### On 6:18 — Covenant (בְּרִית): First Occurrence

וַהֲקִמֹתִי אֶת-בְּרִיתִי אִתָּךְ — "And I will establish my covenant with you." This is the first occurrence of the word בְּרִית ("covenant") in the Bible. The covenant with Noah (elaborated in ch. 9) is the foundational covenant of the post-flood world. Note that YHWH says "my covenant" — it already exists as a divine intention before being formally enacted. The Hiphil of קוּם (וַהֲקִמֹתִי) means "to cause to stand, to establish, to confirm" — not to initiate for the first time but to enact what was already decreed.

The ark's passenger list (v. 18) follows a deliberate structure: Noah / sons / wife / sons' wives. Eight souls (a number the NT will note in 1 Peter 3:20).

### On 6:22 — The Closing Formula

וַיַּעַשׂ נֹחַ כְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר צִוָּה אֹתוֹ אֱלֹהִים כֵּן עָשָׂה.

"And Noah did according to all that God commanded him — so he did."

The closing formula is doubled: כְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר צִוָּה ("according to all that he commanded") is then restated as כֵּן עָשָׂה ("so he did"). This redundant affirmation is not stylistic padding — it is the narrator's emphatic underscoring of total obedience. In a chapter of total corruption, Noah's total compliance is the counter-weight. The formula will be repeated at the close of ch. 7's account of embarkation.

The shift from יְהוָה (YHWH) to אֱלֹהִים (Elohim) in the commanding voice of v. 22 reflects the alternation that runs through ch. 6: vv. 5-8 use YHWH (the personal name); vv. 9-22 use Elohim (the universal divine title). Classical source criticism assigns the YHWH sections to J and the Elohim sections to P; EMET notes the alternation without adjudicating the source question, observing only that the names are used consistently with their established profiles: YHWH for relational pathos and grief; Elohim for cosmic order and commanding authority.

---

### LXX Comparison Summary for Genesis 6

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 6:2 | בְּנֵי-הָאֱלֹהִים | υἱοὶ τοῦ θεοῦ | Vaticanus: "sons of God." Other LXX mss: "angels of God" |
| 6:3 | לֹא-יָדוֹן | οὐ μὴ καταμείνῃ | LXX reads "shall not remain/abide" — supports nuance #2 |
| 6:4 | הַנְּפִלִים | οἱ γίγαντες | LXX "giants" — major interpretive expansion |
| 6:6 | וַיִּתְעַצֵּב אֶל-לִבּוֹ | καὶ διενοήθη | LXX softens grief to contemplation |
| 6:9 | הִתְהַלֶּךְ-נֹחַ אֶת-הָאֱלֹהִים | τῷ θεῷ εὐηρέστησεν Νωε | LXX: "Noah pleased God" — loses the "walking" idiom |
| 6:14 | עֲצֵי-גֹפֶר | ξύλα τετράγωνα | LXX: "squared timbers" — interprets by shape, not species |
| 6:16 | צֹהַר | ἐπισυνάγων | LXX equally uncertain; renders structurally |
| 6:17 | מַבּוּל | κατακλυσμόν | "kataklusmos" — the term that becomes English "cataclysm" |
